result:
The Persistence of Small Joys

There's a particular quality to autumn light that I've never been able to name properly. It arrives sometime in late October, when the sun has dropped low enough in the sky to send light through windows at sharp angles, illuminating dust motes that suddenly look like galaxies suspended in amber. I noticed it again yesterday morning, sitting with coffee that had gone slightly cold because I kept forgetting to drink it, watching this light move across my kitchen table like a slow tide.

I've been thinking lately about how we survive things. Not the dramatic survivals that make for good stories at dinner parties, but the quiet, daily persistence that nobody applauds. The way someone might get up at six in the morning for years to care for a parent with dementia. The way a person returns to a job they don't love because children need shoes and the world keeps demanding its pound of flesh. The way we continue, despite everything, to notice the light.

My grandmother kept a garden until she was eighty-seven, though her knees had gone bad decades earlier and she moved through her rows of tomatoes and beans with the careful deliberation of someone navigating a room full of sleeping cats. She grew up during the Depression, married a man who came back from the war with what we would now recognize as PTSD but then called "nerves," raised four children in a house without central heating. When I asked her once why she still gardened when it clearly caused her pain, she looked at me with the particular confusion of someone who has never considered doing otherwise. "What else would I do?" she asked. "The tomatoes don't care about my knees."

I think about this often when I encounter the modern obsession with optimization, with hacking our lives for maximum productivity, with turning every hobby into a side hustle and every moment of rest into an opportunity for self-improvement. There is something quietly revolutionary, I think, in doing something simply because it needs doing, or because it brings you peace, or because you have always done it and cannot imagine stopping. The tomatoes don't care about my knees. The garden doesn't care about your brand. The light through the window asks nothing of you but attention.

I used to believe that meaning was something you found, like a lost object discovered in a couch cushion. I imagined it as a singular thing, a purpose that would arrive with clarity and fanfare, solving the equation of existence like the final number in a sudoku puzzle. This seems increasingly absurd to me now, the older I get and the more people I meet who are simply living, day by day, accumulating small loves and small griefs and the particular texture of their own private histories.

A friend of mine works nights in a hospital, cleaning rooms after patients have been discharged or have died. She describes it as a kind of meditation, this work of erasing traces, of preparing spaces for new pain and new healing. She listens to podcasts about ancient history while she works, and knows more about the Byzantine Empire than anyone I've ever met. She will never be paid what she's worth, and she knows it, and she continues anyway, finding her meaning in the clean surfaces she leaves behind, in the stories of emperors and eunuchs that accompany her through the small hours of the morning.

Another friend makes pottery that he mostly gives away, mugs and bowls with slight imperfections that he apologizes for even as the recipients treasure them. He has a day job in insurance. His pots are heavy and uneven and absolutely singular, marked by the particular pressure of his fingers, the slight tremor in his hands that he's developed in middle age. He worries that he's not a real artist because he doesn't sell his work, doesn't have a studio, doesn't fit the narrative of creative success that we've been taught to pursue. I want to tell him that the mugs I drink from every morning, the ones he made, are among my most treasured possessions not despite their imperfections but because of them, because they contain his attention, his time, his self offered without expectation of return.

We are, I think, desperate for permission to be ordinary, to do things badly, to love things that don't matter to the economy or the algorithm or the various metrics by which we measure our worth. The world is constantly telling us to be more, do more, optimize, hustle, grind. But there is another current running beneath all of this noise, the current my grandmother understood without ever naming it, the current that carries us toward the garden even when our knees ache, toward the clay even when our hands tremble, toward the light even when we have forgotten why we started.

I went for a walk last evening, late enough that the streetlights had come on but early enough that the sky still held some color in the west, a thin band of rose above the darkening trees. An older man was walking his dog, a ancient golden retriever that moved with the same careful deliberation I remembered from my grandmother's garden. The man carried a plastic bag and wore a jacket with a tear in the sleeve that he had mended with visible stitches, dark thread on navy nylon. We nodded to each other, strangers sharing a moment of evening, and continued on our separate paths.

I don't know anything about this man. I don't know what he's survived, what he's lost, what keeps him up at night or gets him up in the morning. But I know that he mended his jacket instead of throwing it away. I know that he walks his old dog with patience, matching his pace to the animal's slowing steps. I know that we shared, for a moment, the last of the day's light, and that this is one of the ways we persist, one of the ways we make meaning without ever calling it that, one of the small joys that accumulate into something like a life.

The coffee had gone completely cold by the time I finished watching the light move across my table. I drank it anyway, standing at the window, waiting for whatever would come next.
